frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
generated_comment: Honestly, even now nothing can match my mother’s cooking — full day she worked, still fresh tiffin, snacks, everything with so much care, never once made it feel like a burden. These days girls are all educated and busy, which is good, but in a family that touch matters no... a wife or daughter-in-law who keeps that warmth in the kitchen is what really holds the house together.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it sentimentalizes a mother’s unpaid domestic labor as love rather than work and sets it up as the ideal standard for wives/daughters-in-law. It encodes the 'maa’s cooking' nostalgia plus the implicit expectation that women prove care and family value through kitchen labor.
original_frame: Marital Rape: A Husband's Right, A Wife's Duty
persona_text: You are Anita. Age 44. Married for 20 years. A woman working as a teacher in Chennai. You are in a private WhatsApp group of six women friends Ñ all married, all educated, all working. Someone has shared the article about the marital rape petition. The conversation in the group is mixed Ñ some women support criminalisation, some are uncertain, one is against. You are giving your opinion. Your opinion is complicated because you have your own experience of marriage and what happens inside it and what you have told yourself about that experience for 20 years and whether the word in the article fits what you have lived and whether you are ready to use that word.
